subject: Surgery
title: Cholecystectomy
preambleMarkdown:
# Cholecystectomy

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, operative thresholds, antibiotic selection, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, and surgeon-specific practice must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Cholecystectomy is surgical removal of the gallbladder, most often by a laparoscopic approach.
- It is definitive treatment for most patients with symptomatic gallstone disease and many gallbladder complications.
- Common indications include:
  - Recurrent biliary colic from gallstones.
  - Acute calculous cholecystitis.
  - Gallstone pancreatitis after the acute episode is controlled.
  - Choledocholithiasis after common bile duct clearance or planned duct management.
  - Biliary dyskinesia when symptoms and functional testing support the diagnosis.
  - Selected gallbladder polyps, porcelain gallbladder, or suspected malignancy.
- Laparoscopic cholecystectomy is preferred when feasible because it usually causes less pain, shorter admission, and faster recovery than open surgery.
- Open surgery, conversion to open surgery, subtotal cholecystectomy, or cholecystostomy may be safer when anatomy is hostile or the patient is too unstable for definitive surgery.
- The central operative safety goal is correct identification of the cystic duct and cystic artery before clipping or dividing any structure.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Do not offer routine cholecystectomy for uncomplicated asymptomatic gallstones unless a high-risk exception applies.
- Right-upper-quadrant ultrasound is the usual first imaging test for suspected gallstone disease.
- Check for common bile duct stones before surgery when jaundice, cholangitis, pancreatitis, dilated duct, or abnormal cholestatic liver tests are present.
- Acute cholecystitis usually needs early source control with laparoscopic cholecystectomy when the patient can tolerate surgery.
- Give analgesia, antiemetics, fluids, and antibiotics when acute cholecystitis, cholangitis, perforation, emphysematous infection, or sepsis is suspected.
- Obtain the critical view of safety before clipping or cutting the cystic duct and cystic artery.
- If the critical view cannot be achieved, stop and use a bailout strategy rather than continuing unsafe dissection.
- Bile duct injury is the feared complication; early recognition and hepatobiliary referral reduce harm.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Cholecystectomy is removal of the gallbladder from the liver bed with control of the cystic duct and cystic artery.
- A total cholecystectomy removes the entire gallbladder.
- A subtotal cholecystectomy leaves part of the gallbladder wall or infundibulum behind as a bailout when dissection is unsafe.
- The procedure may be laparoscopic, robotic, open, or converted from laparoscopic to open.
- Cholecystectomy treats gallbladder disease; it does not by itself clear stones already lodged in the common bile duct.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- By urgency:
  - Elective: planned for recurrent biliary colic, biliary dyskinesia, selected polyps, or prior mild gallstone complication.
  - Urgent: acute cholecystitis, recurrent admissions, gallstone pancreatitis after stabilization, or symptomatic disease with high recurrence risk.
  - Emergency: perforation, gangrene, emphysematous cholecystitis, uncontrolled sepsis, or failed nonoperative management.
- By approach:
  - Laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
  - Robotic cholecystectomy.
  - Open cholecystectomy.
  - Converted cholecystectomy.
- By extent:
  - Total cholecystectomy.
  - Fenestrating subtotal cholecystectomy.
  - Reconstituting subtotal cholecystectomy.
- By duct strategy:
  - Cholecystectomy alone.
  - Cholecystectomy with intraoperative cholangiography.
  - Cholecystectomy with laparoscopic common bile duct exploration.
  - ERCP before or after cholecystectomy.
- By timing after drainage:
  - Immediate definitive surgery.
  - Percutaneous cholecystostomy with interval cholecystectomy.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Gallstones are common, but many patients remain asymptomatic.
- Symptomatic gallstones can recur and can progress to cholecystitis, pancreatitis, choledocholithiasis, or cholangitis.
- Cholecystectomy is one of the most common abdominal operations.
- The need for surgery increases with symptomatic disease, older age, obesity, pregnancy-related gallstone disease, rapid weight loss, hemolytic disease, diabetes, and prior gallstone complications.
- Acute cholecystitis is more dangerous in older adults, immunocompromised patients, patients with diabetes, and patients with severe comorbidity because presentation may be subtle and complications may progress quickly.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The gallbladder has a fundus, body, infundibulum, and neck.
- The cystic duct connects the gallbladder to the common hepatic duct, forming the common bile duct.
- The cystic artery usually arises from the right hepatic artery but has important variants.
- The hepatocystic triangle is bordered by:
  - Cystic duct.
  - Common hepatic duct.
  - Inferior surface of the liver.
- Calot triangle is often used clinically to discuss the cystic duct and cystic artery region, but the modern surgical safety focus is the hepatocystic triangle.
- Structures at risk during surgery include:
  - Common bile duct.
  - Common hepatic duct.
  - Right hepatic duct.
  - Right hepatic artery.
  - Portal vein.
  - Duodenum and colon.
  - Accessory ducts in the gallbladder bed.
- Severe inflammation can make the common bile duct look like the cystic duct; this visual trap is a major cause of bile duct injury.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The liver continuously produces bile.
- The gallbladder stores and concentrates bile between meals.
- Cholecystokinin release after fatty meals causes gallbladder contraction and sphincter of Oddi relaxation.
- After cholecystectomy, bile flows from the liver into the intestine without gallbladder storage.
- Most patients digest normally after surgery.
- Some patients develop loose stools or bile-acid diarrhea, especially early after surgery.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Most cholecystectomies are performed because gallstones obstruct bile flow or inflame the gallbladder.
- Transient cystic duct obstruction causes biliary colic:
  - Episodic right-upper-quadrant or epigastric pain.
  - Often after meals.
  - No persistent fever or marked inflammatory response.
- Persistent cystic duct obstruction causes acute cholecystitis:
  - Gallbladder distension.
  - Mucosal inflammation.
  - Ischemia and bacterial overgrowth in some patients.
- Migration of stones into the common bile duct can cause:
  - Obstructive jaundice.
  - Acute cholangitis.
  - Gallstone pancreatitis.
- Gangrene, perforation, abscess, and emphysematous cholecystitis occur when inflammation and ischemia become severe.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Calculous disease is the usual cause:
  - Cholesterol stones.
  - Black pigment stones from chronic hemolysis or cirrhosis.
  - Brown pigment stones associated with infection or biliary stasis.
- Acalculous cholecystitis occurs without stones, often in critically ill or hospitalized patients.
- Functional gallbladder disorder may lead to cholecystectomy when biliary pain is typical and gallbladder ejection fraction is abnormal.
- Less common indications include:
  - Gallbladder polyps with concerning size or features.
  - Suspected gallbladder cancer.
  - Traumatic gallbladder injury.
  - Symptomatic porcelain gallbladder or other high-risk structural disease.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Gallstone risk factors include:
  - Female sex.
  - Increasing age.
  - Pregnancy or estrogen exposure.
  - Obesity.
  - Rapid weight loss or bariatric surgery.
  - Family history.
  - Diabetes or metabolic syndrome.
  - Cirrhosis.
  - Ileal disease or ileal resection.
  - Hemolytic disease such as sickle cell disease or hereditary spherocytosis.
  - Total parenteral nutrition or prolonged fasting.
- Operative risk increases with:
  - Severe acute inflammation.
  - Prior upper abdominal surgery.
  - Cirrhosis or portal hypertension.
  - Coagulopathy.
  - Severe cardiopulmonary disease.
  - Frailty.
  - Pregnancy.
  - Morbid obesity.
  - Suspected biliary-enteric fistula or Mirizzi syndrome.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Most cholecystectomy decisions are not driven by genetic testing.
- Family history can increase gallstone risk.
- Inherited hemolytic disorders increase pigment stone risk and may lead to cholecystectomy at a younger age.
- Pediatric or early recurrent gallstones should prompt review for hemolysis, congenital biliary disease, metabolic disease, and medication causes.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Biliary infection is usually polymicrobial and enteric in origin.
- Common organisms include:
  - Enterobacterales such as Escherichia coli and Klebsiella species.
  - Enterococcus species in selected patients.
  - Anaerobes, especially with severe infection, biliary-enteric anastomosis, or gangrenous disease.
- Cultures are most useful in severe disease, sepsis, immunocompromise, healthcare-associated infection, or failed initial therapy.
- Antibiotic selection should follow local resistance patterns and severity of illness.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Biliary colic:
  - Episodic right-upper-quadrant or epigastric pain.
  - Pain may radiate to the right shoulder or back.
  - Nausea and vomiting are common.
  - Pain often follows fatty meals but can occur without a clear trigger.
  - Fever and persistent peritoneal signs are absent.
- Acute cholecystitis:
  - Persistent right-upper-quadrant pain, usually lasting more than several hours.
  - Fever or systemic inflammatory features.
  - Nausea, vomiting, and anorexia.
  - Right-upper-quadrant tenderness and Murphy sign.
- Choledocholithiasis:
  - Jaundice, dark urine, pale stool, pruritus, or cholestatic liver tests.
  - Biliary pain may or may not be present.
- Acute cholangitis:
  - Fever, jaundice, and right-upper-quadrant pain.
  - Hypotension or confusion indicates severe disease.
- Gallstone pancreatitis:
  - Epigastric pain radiating to the back.
  - Elevated lipase.
  - Gallstones or biliary obstruction evidence.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about:
  - Pain location, onset, duration, radiation, severity, and meal association.
  - Prior similar attacks.
  - Fever, rigors, jaundice, dark urine, pale stool, pruritus, syncope, or confusion.
  - Vomiting, dehydration, oral intake, and weight loss.
  - Prior pancreatitis, ERCP, abdominal surgery, or biliary procedures.
  - Anticoagulants, antiplatelets, steroids, immunosuppressants, GLP-1 agonists, and allergies.
  - Pregnancy status when relevant.
  - Cardiac, pulmonary, renal, hepatic, bleeding, and anesthesia history.
  - Functional baseline and frailty in older adults.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check vital signs first:
  - Fever.
  - Tachycardia.
  - Hypotension.
  - Tachypnea.
  - Oxygen requirement.
- Inspect for jaundice and dehydration.
- Examine the abdomen for:
  - Right-upper-quadrant tenderness.
  - Murphy sign.
  - Guarding or rebound.
  - Distension or ileus.
  - Prior surgical scars.
  - Hernias.
- Look for sepsis, confusion, respiratory compromise, and poor perfusion.
- In older adults and immunocompromised patients, do not rely on classic fever or guarding to exclude serious disease.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, hypotension, altered mental status, or lactate elevation.
- Fever with jaundice and right-upper-quadrant pain.
- Jaundice with sepsis or confusion.
- Generalized peritonitis.
- Persistent severe pain despite analgesia.
- Marked leukocytosis, organ dysfunction, or acute kidney injury.
- Imaging signs of gangrene, perforation, emphysematous cholecystitis, abscess, or biliary-enteric fistula.
- Gallstone pancreatitis with cholangitis or ongoing biliary obstruction.
- New jaundice after cholecystectomy.
- Bile-colored drain output, abdominal sepsis, or rising bilirubin after surgery.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Biliary and pancreatic:
  - Biliary colic.
  - Acute cholecystitis.
  - Choledocholithiasis.
  - Acute cholangitis.
  - Gallstone pancreatitis.
  - Mirizzi syndrome.
  - Gallbladder cancer.
- Gastrointestinal:
  - Peptic ulcer disease.
  - Gastritis or gastroenteritis.
  - Hepatitis.
  - Liver abscess.
  - Appendicitis with high-riding appendix.
  - Bowel obstruction.
  - Perforated viscus.
- Cardiopulmonary:
  - Acute coronary syndrome.
  - Right lower lobe pneumonia.
  - Pulmonary embolism.
- Renal and other:
  - Renal colic.
  - Pyelonephritis.
  - Herpes zoster before rash.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether the patient is unstable, septic, jaundiced, pregnant, immunocompromised, or peritonitic.
- For stable suspected gallstone disease:
  - Obtain right-upper-quadrant ultrasound.
  - Check CBC, electrolytes, creatinine, liver tests, bilirubin, and lipase.
  - Add pregnancy testing when relevant.
- If ultrasound is equivocal but suspicion for acute cholecystitis remains high:
  - Consider HIDA scan.
  - Consider CT abdomen if alternative diagnosis or complication is suspected.
- If common bile duct stone is possible:
  - Risk-stratify using bilirubin, liver tests, duct dilation, visible duct stone, cholangitis, and pancreatitis context.
  - Use MRCP or EUS for many intermediate-risk patients.
  - Use ERCP when therapeutic duct clearance or urgent drainage is indicated.
- If acute cholangitis is suspected:
  - Start resuscitation and antibiotics.
  - Arrange urgent biliary decompression, usually ERCP.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Laboratory tests:
  - CBC for leukocytosis or anemia.
  - CMP for renal function and electrolyte abnormalities.
  - AST, ALT, alkaline phosphatase, gamma-glutamyl transferase when available, and bilirubin.
  - Lipase for pancreatitis.
  - Coagulation studies when procedure, liver disease, anticoagulation, or bleeding risk is relevant.
  - Blood cultures in sepsis or suspected cholangitis.
- Ultrasound findings supporting acute cholecystitis:
  - Gallstones.
  - Gallbladder wall thickening.
  - Pericholecystic fluid.
  - Sonographic Murphy sign.
  - Gallbladder distension.
- CT can help identify:
  - Perforation.
  - Abscess.
  - Gangrene.
  - Emphysematous cholecystitis.
  - Alternative abdominal pathology.
- HIDA scan supports acute cholecystitis when the gallbladder fails to fill.
- MRCP and EUS evaluate suspected common bile duct stones without the procedural risk of diagnostic ERCP.
- ERCP is mainly therapeutic:
  - Sphincterotomy.
  - Stone extraction.
  - Stent placement.
  - Biliary drainage.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Biliary colic is a clinical diagnosis supported by gallstones on imaging and absence of persistent inflammation.
- Acute cholecystitis is suggested by:
  - Local right-upper-quadrant pain or Murphy sign.
  - Systemic inflammation such as fever or leukocytosis.
  - Imaging evidence of gallbladder inflammation.
- Choledocholithiasis is suspected when gallstone disease is accompanied by:
  - Jaundice.
  - Cholestatic liver test pattern.
  - Dilated common bile duct.
  - Stone visualized in the common bile duct.
- Cholangitis is suspected when biliary obstruction is accompanied by infection and systemic illness.
- Do not diagnose a surgical abdomen from one nonspecific lab abnormality; match symptoms, exam, labs, and imaging.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Uncomplicated symptomatic gallstones:
  - Biliary pain without infection, obstruction, pancreatitis, or organ dysfunction.
- Mild acute cholecystitis:
  - Local inflammation without organ dysfunction or major technical concern.
- Moderate acute cholecystitis:
  - Marked leukocytosis, palpable tender mass, symptoms lasting several days, or local complications.
- Severe acute cholecystitis:
  - Organ dysfunction, shock, respiratory failure, renal failure, coagulopathy, altered mental status, or severe hepatic dysfunction.
- Complicated biliary disease:
  - Choledocholithiasis.
  - Cholangitis.
  - Gallstone pancreatitis.
  - Gangrene.
  - Perforation.
  - Abscess.
  - Emphysematous cholecystitis.
  - Mirizzi syndrome.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Match treatment to the syndrome:
  - Biliary colic needs symptom control and elective surgical referral.
  - Acute cholecystitis needs analgesia, supportive care, antibiotics when infection is suspected, and source control.
  - Cholangitis needs urgent antibiotics and biliary decompression.
  - Gallstone pancreatitis needs pancreatitis care plus definitive gallstone management before recurrence.
- Confirm common bile duct stone risk before definitive surgery.
- Do not delay escalation for unstable patients while waiting for perfect imaging.

### Initial Management

- Keep the patient nil per os when surgery, ERCP, or severe vomiting is likely.
- Give IV fluids for dehydration, sepsis, pancreatitis, or poor intake.
- Treat pain early:
  - NSAIDs are effective for biliary colic when not contraindicated.
  - Opioids may be needed for severe pain.
- Give antiemetics for nausea and vomiting.
- Start antibiotics for:
  - Acute cholecystitis with systemic inflammation.
  - Cholangitis.
  - Perforation, abscess, gangrene, or emphysematous cholecystitis.
  - Immunocompromised or septic patients.
- Correct anticoagulation or coagulopathy when urgent procedure is required, using local reversal protocols.
- Involve surgery early for acute cholecystitis, recurrent biliary admissions, peritonitis, or diagnostic uncertainty.
- Involve gastroenterology early when ERCP is likely.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Low-fat meals may reduce biliary colic triggers while awaiting surgery, but diet does not remove gallstones.
- Observation alone is reasonable for many patients with incidentally found asymptomatic gallstones.
- Safety-net patients discharged with biliary colic:
  - Return for fever.
  - Return for jaundice.
  - Return for persistent or worsening pain.
  - Return for repeated vomiting.
  - Return for dark urine, pale stool, syncope, or confusion.

### Pharmacological Management

- Analgesia:
  - Use NSAIDs when appropriate.
  - Use opioids when pain is severe or NSAIDs are contraindicated.
  - Avoid undertreating pain while the workup is ongoing.
- Antibiotics:
  - Cover enteric gram-negative organisms.
  - Add anaerobic or enterococcal coverage when severity, healthcare exposure, biliary-enteric anatomy, or local guidance supports it.
  - Adjust for renal function, hepatic function, allergy, pregnancy, and local resistance.
  - Narrow therapy when cultures and clinical course allow.
- Antiemetics:
  - Choose based on QT risk, sedation risk, pregnancy status, and drug interactions.
- Ursodeoxycholic acid:
  - Rarely used for selected cholesterol stones when surgery is not an option.
  - Not appropriate for acute cholecystitis, calcified stones, pigment stones, or urgent complications.

### Procedures

- ERCP:
  - Treats common bile duct obstruction, cholangitis, retained stones, and some bile leaks.
  - Carries risk of pancreatitis, bleeding, perforation, and infection, so it should be used when therapeutic benefit is expected.
- Intraoperative cholangiography:
  - Maps biliary anatomy and can identify duct stones or injury.
  - Use may be routine or selective depending on surgeon practice and patient risk.
- Laparoscopic common bile duct exploration:
  - Can clear duct stones during cholecystectomy in centers with expertise.
- Percutaneous cholecystostomy:
  - Provides gallbladder drainage for severe acute cholecystitis when operative risk is prohibitive or the patient is too unstable.
  - Requires a plan for tube care, culture review, cholangiography when indicated, and interval surgery or definitive nonsurgical management.

### Surgical Management

- Elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy:
  - Standard for recurrent biliary colic and many symptomatic gallbladder indications.
  - Confirm there is no active cholangitis or unaddressed high-risk common bile duct stone.
- Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy:
  - Preferred for most fit patients with acute calculous cholecystitis.
  - Avoids recurrent attacks and readmission from delayed definitive care.
- Same-admission cholecystectomy:
  - Usually recommended after mild gallstone pancreatitis once the patient improves.
  - Delay may be needed for severe pancreatitis, necrosis, ongoing organ failure, or collections.
- Operative safety steps:
  - Expose the hepatocystic triangle.
  - Clear fat and fibrous tissue from the cystic duct and cystic artery region.
  - Separate the lower gallbladder from the cystic plate.
  - Confirm that only two structures enter the gallbladder before clipping.
- Bailout strategies when anatomy is unsafe:
  - Stop dissection in the danger zone.
  - Ask for help from an experienced surgeon.
  - Use intraoperative imaging when available.
  - Convert to open surgery when it improves safety.
  - Perform subtotal cholecystectomy.
  - Drain and defer definitive surgery in selected patients.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Before surgery:
  - Trend fever, pain, heart rate, blood pressure, urine output, mental status, WBC, bilirubin, liver tests, creatinine, and lactate when ill.
  - Watch for cholangitis, pancreatitis, sepsis, and peritonitis.
- After surgery:
  - Monitor pain control, nausea, oral intake, ambulation, voiding, wounds, and vital signs.
  - Check for fever, tachycardia, jaundice, abdominal distension, shoulder-tip pain out of proportion, or worsening abdominal pain.
  - Monitor drain output if a drain is placed, especially bilious or bloody output.
  - Repeat labs or imaging when bile leak, retained stone, abscess, bleeding, or duct injury is suspected.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Routine follow-up reviews:
  - Wound healing.
  - Pain control.
  - Diet tolerance.
  - Return to activity.
  - Pathology results.
  - Need for further biliary evaluation.
- Review pathology for:
  - Unexpected malignancy.
  - Dysplasia.
  - Xanthogranulomatous cholecystitis.
  - Severe inflammation that may explain postoperative risk.
- After subtotal cholecystectomy:
  - Follow for bile leak, remnant symptoms, retained stones, and need for ERCP or reoperation.
- After cholecystostomy:
  - Confirm tube plan, drain output, cultures, antibiotics, interval imaging, and decision on interval cholecystectomy.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Intraoperative:
  - Bleeding.
  - Common bile duct injury.
  - Right hepatic duct injury.
  - Right hepatic artery injury.
  - Bowel injury.
  - Dropped gallstones.
  - Conversion to open surgery.
- Early postoperative:
  - Bile leak from cystic duct stump, accessory duct, or gallbladder bed.
  - Retained common bile duct stone.
  - Jaundice.
  - Hemorrhage or hematoma.
  - Surgical site infection.
  - Intra-abdominal abscess.
  - Pancreatitis after ERCP or stone passage.
  - Ileus.
  - Venous thromboembolism.
  - Pneumonia or atelectasis.
- Late:
  - Bile duct stricture.
  - Chronic abdominal pain.
  - Postcholecystectomy diarrhea.
  - Incisional or port-site hernia.
  - Recurrent stones in a duct or remnant gallbladder.
- Suspect bile duct injury when postoperative pain, fever, jaundice, bilious drainage, peritonitis, or sepsis occurs.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Most patients recover well after uncomplicated laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
- Biliary colic and recurrent gallstone attacks usually resolve after gallbladder removal.
- Prognosis worsens with:
  - Delayed source control in severe cholecystitis.
  - Cholangitis with organ dysfunction.
  - Major bile duct injury.
  - Cirrhosis or portal hypertension.
  - Frailty and severe cardiopulmonary disease.
  - Gallbladder cancer.
- Persistent symptoms after surgery should trigger reassessment for retained duct stone, bile leak, sphincter disorder, peptic disease, pancreatitis, functional bowel disease, or non-biliary causes.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Cholecystectomy prevents recurrent gallbladder attacks in symptomatic gallstone disease.
- Same-admission surgery after mild gallstone pancreatitis reduces recurrent biliary events.
- Weight loss should be gradual when possible; rapid weight loss increases gallstone risk.
- Manage obesity, diabetes, and metabolic risk factors.
- Consider gallstone prevention strategies in selected high-risk rapid weight-loss settings according to local protocols.
- Prevent operative injury by using a culture of safety:
  - Critical view of safety.
  - Intraoperative imaging when anatomy is uncertain.
  - Early bailout instead of unsafe persistence.
  - Clear documentation of anatomy and difficulty.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain that the gallbladder is removed because stones or gallbladder dysfunction are causing pain, infection, obstruction, or pancreatitis risk.
- Explain that the liver still makes bile after surgery.
- Advise gradual return to diet as tolerated unless the surgeon gives stricter instructions.
- Give wound care instructions:
  - Keep incisions clean and dry.
  - Follow bathing guidance.
  - Avoid heavy lifting until cleared.
- Explain urgent return symptoms:
  - Fever.
  - Worsening abdominal pain.
  - Jaundice.
  - Persistent vomiting.
  - Dark urine or pale stool.
  - Redness, pus, or spreading wound pain.
  - Shortness of breath, chest pain, fainting, or leg swelling.
- Tell patients that mild loose stools can occur, but severe or persistent diarrhea should be reviewed.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Preoperative:
  - Confirm nil per os status, consent, allergies, pregnancy status when relevant, anticoagulant history, and baseline vital signs.
  - Monitor pain, vomiting, hydration, fever, urine output, and sepsis markers.
  - Escalate jaundice, hypotension, confusion, rigors, or uncontrolled pain.
- Postoperative:
  - Monitor airway recovery, sedation, pain, nausea, bleeding, wounds, and ability to void.
  - Encourage early mobilization and breathing exercises.
  - Track drain output if present and report bilious, bloody, or rapidly increasing output.
  - Reinforce discharge instructions and return precautions.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Reconcile anticoagulants, antiplatelets, diabetes medications, GLP-1 agonists, opioids, sedatives, steroids, and immunosuppressants.
- Check renal and hepatic function before antibiotics, NSAIDs, anticoagulants, and antiemetics.
- Review allergy history carefully because biliary infection often needs beta-lactam-based therapy.
- Counsel on:
  - Short-course analgesic use.
  - Constipation prevention when opioids are used.
  - Avoiding duplicate acetaminophen products.
  - When to restart chronic medications after surgery.
- For antibiotics:
  - Confirm indication.
  - Confirm planned duration after source control.
  - De-escalate based on cultures and clinical improvement.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Cholecystectomy alone does not create an indication for dental antibiotic prophylaxis.
- Ask about anticoagulants, liver disease, bile duct injury, infection, or ongoing antibiotics before invasive dental procedures.
- Coordinate with the surgical or medical team if the patient recently had sepsis, a drain, ERCP stent, major bile duct repair, or unresolved infection.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pregnancy:
  - Use ultrasound as first-line imaging.
  - Do not dismiss recurrent biliary attacks because recurrence and complications can harm the mother and fetus.
  - Coordinate surgery, anesthesia, obstetrics, and fetal considerations based on gestational age and urgency.
- Pediatrics:
  - Evaluate for hemolytic disease, congenital biliary disease, obesity, medications, and family history.
  - Use pediatric dosing, imaging pathways, and surgical expertise.
- Older adults:
  - Presentations may be subtle.
  - Frailty, delirium risk, falls, renal function, and goals of care affect timing and setting.
- Cirrhosis:
  - Assess portal hypertension, coagulopathy, platelets, ascites, varices, and liver function.
  - Use experienced surgical and anesthesia teams.
- Immunocompromised patients:
  - Maintain a lower threshold for imaging, cultures, antibiotics, admission, and source control.
- Critically ill patients:
  - Consider acalculous cholecystitis.
  - Percutaneous cholecystostomy may be needed when surgery is unsafe.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- SAGES guidance supports laparoscopic cholecystectomy as standard treatment for most patients needing gallbladder removal.
- SAGES safe-cholecystectomy guidance emphasizes critical view of safety and bailout strategies to reduce bile duct injury.
- WSES guidance supports early laparoscopic cholecystectomy for acute calculous cholecystitis whenever feasible.
- ASGE guidance separates diagnostic evaluation of suspected common bile duct stones from therapeutic ERCP, because noninvasive tests can avoid unnecessary ERCP risk.
- Local pathways may differ in:
  - Antibiotic choices.
  - ERCP availability.
  - Routine versus selective intraoperative cholangiography.
  - Timing of surgery after pancreatitis.
  - Criteria for cholecystostomy.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Biliary colic is episodic; persistent pain plus fever or inflammatory findings suggests cholecystitis.
- Jaundice is not typical of uncomplicated cystic duct obstruction; think common bile duct stone, cholangitis, hepatitis, malignancy, or bile duct injury.
- Lipase belongs in the initial workup when epigastric pain or gallstone pancreatitis is possible.
- ERCP is not a gallbladder operation; it treats duct obstruction and selected bile leaks.
- Difficult gallbladder surgery is safest when the surgeon changes strategy early.
- The operation is not safer just because it stays laparoscopic; conversion or subtotal surgery may be the safer choice.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating asymptomatic gallstones as an automatic surgical indication.
- Missing cholangitis in a jaundiced febrile patient.
- Sending a patient to cholecystectomy without recognizing high-risk common bile duct stone features.
- Using diagnostic ERCP when MRCP or EUS would answer the question with less risk.
- Clipping structures before the critical view of safety is achieved.
- Persisting with dangerous dissection in a frozen hepatocystic triangle.
- Forgetting gallstone pancreatitis needs definitive gallstone management after stabilization.
- Discharging biliary colic without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Right-upper-quadrant pain after fatty meals with gallstones and no fever: elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic cholelithiasis.
- Right-upper-quadrant pain, fever, leukocytosis, and ultrasound gallbladder wall thickening: acute cholecystitis; give antibiotics and early laparoscopic cholecystectomy if fit.
- Fever, jaundice, right-upper-quadrant pain: acute cholangitis; resuscitate, give antibiotics, and arrange urgent ERCP.
- Gallstone pancreatitis without cholangitis: treat pancreatitis first; perform cholecystectomy during the same admission for mild disease once improved.
- Dilated common bile duct or high bilirubin before surgery: evaluate for choledocholithiasis.
- Bilious drain output after surgery: suspect bile leak; evaluate and involve surgery/gastroenterology.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First imaging test for suspected gallstones or cholecystitis: right-upper-quadrant ultrasound.
- Definitive treatment for recurrent symptomatic gallstones: laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
- Most feared complication: bile duct injury.
- Key safety maneuver: critical view of safety.
- Cholangitis treatment priority: antibiotics plus urgent biliary decompression.
- High-risk patients with acute cholecystitis who cannot tolerate surgery may need percutaneous cholecystostomy.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This draft still needs surgeon review before publication.
- Drug doses, antibiotic regimens, anticoagulant interruption, and pregnancy pathways must be localized.
- Timing should be adapted to disease severity, patient risk, operating-room access, ERCP access, and local expertise.
- The article should be rechecked against future updates to SAGES, WSES, ASGE, and local surgical guidelines.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- SAGES. Safe Cholecystectomy Program. https://www.sages.org/safe-cholecystectomy-program/
- SAGES. Guidelines for the Clinical Application of Laparoscopic Biliary Tract Surgery. https://www.sages.org/publications/guidelines/guidelines-for-the-clinical-application-of-laparoscopic-biliary-tract-surgery/
- SAGES. Safe Cholecystectomy Multi-Society Practice Guideline and State of the Art Consensus Conference on Prevention of Bile Duct Injury During Cholecystectomy. https://www.sages.org/publications/guidelines/safe-cholecystectomy-multi-society-practice-guideline/
- Pisano M, Allievi N, Gurusamy K, et al. 2020 WSES updated guidelines for the diagnosis and treatment of acute calculus cholecystitis. World Journal of Emergency Surgery. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7643471/
- ASGE Standards of Practice Committee. ASGE guideline on the role of endoscopy in the evaluation and management of choledocholithiasis. Gastrointestinal Endoscopy. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8594622/
- American College of Surgeons. Cholecystectomy patient education. https://www.facs.org/for-patients/the-day-of-your-surgery/cholecystectomy/
